name: Mistletoe
description: Mistletoe, a semi-parasitic plant that grows on various trees, held immense significance in Celtic mythology and Druidic practices. Revered as a sacred herb, it symbolized life, fertility, healing, and protection. Druids, the priestly class of the Celts, considered mistletoe growing on oak trees particularly potent and performed elaborate rituals to harvest it with a golden sickle, ensuring it never touched the ground, preserving its sacred energy. This ritual, often conducted on the sixth night of the moon, involved sacrificing white bulls and feasting. Mistletoe was believed to possess potent medicinal properties, capable of curing ailments, neutralizing poisons, and promoting fertility in both humans and animals. It was also used in protective amulets and charms to ward off evil spirits and bring good fortune. The plant's association with the winter solstice, a time of darkness and rebirth, further cemented its importance in Celtic cosmology. Mistletoe's white berries were linked to male fertility, and the plant's ability to thrive even in the harsh winter months symbolized the enduring power of life. Beyond its practical and ritual uses, mistletoe served as a symbol of peace and reconciliation; warring tribes would sometimes gather beneath mistletoe branches to declare a truce. The plant's multifaceted symbolism and practical applications ensured its prominent place in Celtic culture and spirituality, making it a powerful symbol of life, death, and rebirth.
subtitle: Sacred herb of life and protection

keyMyths.title: The Druids' Harvest
keyMyths.description: Druids harvested mistletoe with a golden sickle, believing it held potent magical properties when cut from an oak tree. The plant was never allowed to touch the ground to preserve its power.

sources.author: Pliny the Elder
sources.description: Describes the Druidic ritual of harvesting mistletoe.
sources.text: Describes the Druidic ritual of harvesting mistletoe.

sources.author: Miranda Green
sources.work: Animals in Celtic Life and Myth
